title: A SIMPLE APPROACH TO ADVERSARIAL ROBUSTNESS - IN FEW SHOT IMAGE CLASSIFICATION [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## A SIMPLE APPROACH TO ADVERSARIAL ROBUSTNESS - IN FEW SHOT IMAGE CLASSIFICATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Few-shot image classification, where the goal is to generalize to tasks with limited labeled data, has seen great progress over the years. However, the classifiers are vulnerable to adversarial examples, posing a question regarding their generalization capabilities. Recent works have tried to combine meta-learning approaches with adversarial training to improve the robustness of few-shot classifiers. We show that a simple transfer-learning based approach can be used to train adversarially robust few-shot classifiers. We also present a method for novel classification task based on calibrating the centroid of the few-shot category towards the base classes. We show that standard adversarial training on base categories along with centroid-based classifier in the novel categories, outperforms or is on-par with state-of-the-art advanced methods on standard benchmarks such as Mini-ImageNet, CIFAR-FS and CUB datasets. Our method is simple and easy to scale, and with little effort

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Few-shot learning presents the challenge of generalizing to unseen tasks with limited data. The problem is aimed at learning quickly from few examples of data, which is generally considered the hallmark of human intelligence. This is an important practical problem due to the scarce availability of fully annotated data in the real world. Researchers have shown that such a setting can be considered for various real world computer vision tasks such as image classification (Finn et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019), object detection (Wang et al., 2020), image segmentation (Rakelly et al., 2018), facerecognition (Guo et al., 2020) and medical analysis (Maicas et al., 2018). As a result, it is of paramount importance that such safety-critical systems are reliable and robust to changes in input. Specifically in this work, we consider robustness to adversarial examples - carefully crafted perturbations using gradients that when added to inputs, _fool_ the classifier. The most common method of improving robustness is by adversarial training (Goodfellow et al., 2015) which involves training on adversarial examples using adversary of choice. Traditional adversarially robust methods (Madry et al., 2018; Goodfellow et al., 2015; Szegedy et al., 2013) consider a data-rich setting where many examples are available per category. It has also been shown that adversarial generalization possibly requires significantly more data (Schmidt et al., 2018). This becomes challenging in a scenario where the end-user has access to limited amount of annotated data but is interested in building a robust few-shot classifier. Such a setting is more practical and it is important to develop methods which can work with minimal effort in the pre-deployment stage. We show from our experiments that a simple approach of finetuning the network on clean data from an adversarially robust base model can lead to significant improvement in robustness with minimal resources.

Previous works on improving robustness for few-shot classifiers focus mainly on meta-learning approaches. Here the base model is trained on adversarial examples of episodic data. We show that standard adversarial training on a large dataset is sufficient for learning a robust classifier. This makes our method simple and scalable, making the process of training robust classifiers straightforward and also creating directions to explore methods from robustness literature for few-shot setting.

It is important to understand the problem of few-shot learning in order to develop their robust counterparts. The goal in few-shot learning is to learn transferable knowledge for generalization to tasks with limited data. These have generally been partitioned into metric learning (Snell et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2016; Sung et al., 2018), optimization-based (Finn et al., 2017; Ravi & Larochelle,

2016) and hallucination based methods (Hariharan & Girshick, 2017; Yang et al., 2021; Antoniou et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018). The most common work among optimization based methods is MAML (Finn et al., 2017) which aims at learning a network initialization using a bi-level optimization procedure, that when finetuned on limited data is able to generalize to the new task. Recent works have shown that meta-learning methods can be extended to include adversarial robustness as well. (Goldblum et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021) perform adversarial training on top of meta-learners to improve robustness significantly. However, adversarial training on its own is expensive and combining with meta-learning makes the problem computationally intensive. (Wang et al., 2021) showed that there exists a compromise between training robust meta-learners and performance, both in terms of standard and robust accuracy, motivating the need for a simpler approach.

Another interesting line of work, focused on improving few-shot learning, shows that a simple method which involves training on large scale data and finetuning the model on the few-shot dataset can match or even outperform meta-learning methods (Chen et al., 2019; Dhillon et al., 2019). The intuition is that the model sees examples from all categories and can get a general sense of semantics rather than seeing only episodic data. Such a setting also makes it easier to train on large scale data that can lead to further improvements as shown in (Dhillon et al., 2019). Our results show considering a simple setting can be beneficial for adversarial robustness as well.

We consider the few-shot setting and show that adversarial training along with simple nearest centroid based classifier can outperform previous methods in terms of robustness. Such a setting is practically relevant, since the adversarial training on large data needs to be done just once and robustness for few-shot classes can be achieved without creating adversarial examples for the specific task. We believe it also becomes easier to incorporate new approaches to robustness, such as verifiably robust classifiers (Gowal et al., 2018; Cohen et al., 2019) and can bring together robust methods for both large and limited dataset settings.

In the following sections, we describe our method and discuss relevant related work. We present experimental findings and implementation details in the subsequent sections followed by conclusion and directions for future work.

## 2 METHOD

Here we introduce notation and provide a description of our method. Our first objective is to learn a feature extractor _fθb_ and linear classifier _Cωb_ using the abundantly-labeled base dataset _Xb_ . At the next stage, when a _N_ -way _K_ -shot few-shot task is sampled from the novel dataset _Xn_ , we use only the feature extractor _fθb_ and learn a new linear classifier _Cωn_ such that it can generalize to unseen examples from the novel categories. We show that a simple approach of training a robust base model and then adapting it to novel categories can outperform previous approaches. We divide our approach into two stages: (1) Robust Base training and (2) Novel training.

## 2.1 ROBUST BASE TRAINING

Given a base dataset _Xb_ with large number of annotated examples per category, we perform adversarial training using an iterative adversary such as PGD (Madry et al., 2018). Specifically, we solve the min-max objective

Here, _L_ ( _θ, x, y_ ) represents the training objective, which is commonly cross-entropy and _θ_ = ( _θb, ωb_ ) represents the combination of the feature extractor and base classifier parameters. There are different methods for optimizing the inner maximization in Equation 1. For all our experiments we use the Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) algorithm, an iterative algorithm presented in (Madry et al., 2018) with _p_ = _∞_ which corresponds to finding a perturbation _δ_ around an _ϵ_ -bounded hypercube around _x_ that maximizes the objective. Once we find the perturbation, the perturbed input is added to the training set and parameters are tuned. This method is called adversarial training and is the most widely used method to improve robustness to adversarial examples. Note that adversarial training, which is a computationally expensive procedure, needs to be performed just once using base dataset.

In the next stage, we use only clean examples which makes it practical and easy to train robust

**Weight averaging:** Weight averaging (WA) has been shown as a simple way to improve generalization (Izmailov et al., 2018; Garipov et al., 2018) in deep networks as it approximates ensembling in temporal fashion and can find flatter optima in loss surface. This method has been used in adversarial training (Gowal et al., 2020; Chen et al.) for improving robustness in standard classification task. Since we are interested in using base parameters at the next stage, we perform weight averaging for only the feature extractor parameters _θb_ and show this can be used for few-shot setting.

Similar to (Gowal et al., 2020), we keep a separate copy of the weights and for every iteration perform exponential moving average method _θb[′] ← τθb[′]_ + (1 _− τ_ ) _∗ θb_ and use _θb[′]_ during the evaluation. We set _τ_ = 0 _._ 999 in all our experiments.

## 2.2 NOVEL TRAINING

During this stage, we consider the _N_ -way, _K_ -shot method as the novel task and adapt our learnt feature extractor _fθb_ using classifier _Cωn_ . For all our experiments, we found best results when the weights of feature extractor _fθb_ are frozen and not optimized during novel training. Intuitively, this can be understood not wanting the parameters of the feature extractor to be biased towards the fewshot examples. And since we are interested in learning only from clean data during the novel training, there can also be an effect of _forgetting_ the robustness learnt at the base stage. This was observed in (Goldblum et al., 2019) where only the final layer was trained and rest of the parameters were frozen. During the novel training stage, we use only clean examples and not adversarial examples, making the process straightforward.

**Linear classifier:** The simplest possible baseline is to learn a linear model on top of the frozen feature extractor using the few shot examples of novel categories. As shown in our experiments, this simple baseline on its own achieves reasonable performance compared to previous approaches. This baseline also suggests that a robust base classifier corresponds to a robust novel classifier and a simple approach such as ours is enough to achieve robustness for few-shot classifiers. However, as observed in previous works (Wang et al., 2021; Goldblum et al., 2019) and in our experiments, this approach alone is not sufficient to achieve improved robustness. Interestingly, we achieve much closer results to state of the art compared to previous works using this simple baseline. One challenge associated with using few-shot data is that the model can become biased towards the specific samples and may not capture the true class distribution. Hence there is a need for more calibrated classifiers.

**Background on Distribution Calibation (DC) (Yang et al., 2021):** Recent work (Yang et al., 2021) has shown that standard accuracy of few-shot classifiers can be improved by using Distribution Calibration. They present a _free-lunch_ hallucination-based method where the feature distributions of the novel categories are calibrated using the base dataset, due to the similarity between the base and novel datasets. The mean and covariance of each novel category is calibrated using the statistic of base data. They use these statistics to _hallucinate_ or sample many points from a Gaussian distribution, and learn a logistic regression classifier. This simple method was shown to improve standard accuracy significantly under various settings.

## **Mean Calibration and Nearest Centroid (NC):**

DC method can be computationally expensive due to the calculation of covariance matrix which can be of _O_ ( _N ∗ D_[2] ) complexity where _D_ is the dimensionality of the feature space and _N_ is the number of data points in the base dataset. The covariance matrix is also expensive to store in memory. Moreover, sampling from a multivariate Gaussian with non-diagonal covariance is also expensive and can be of the order of at least _O_ ( _D_[2] _[.]_[3] ) (Bishop, 2006). These can reduce the applicability of the approach for certain architectures.

Another aspect of using the hallucinated features is that the model can become biased to the clean features and learn a non-robust final classifier. Note that since these additional data points are generated in the feature space and not the image space, it is not possible to create adversarial versions of these features and perform adversarial training using the large set of features. This poses a problem of improving the performance during novel training without sacrificing robustness.

To overcome these problems, we present a simple method where we rely only on the calibrated mean and classify query sample using a non-parametric Nearest Centroid based algorithm. We find the nearest base-category centers to each novel training sample and then average them along with the novel training sample to obtain the new mean or centroid for the novel category, similar to (Yang et al., 2021). We do not consider the covariance matrix in our method and we find this approximation works equally well in our experiments. More formally:

At the inference time, the Nearest Centroid (NC) method requires less memory and computation compared to a Nearest Neighbor classifier since we only have to store and compare with one prototype per class rather than the entire training set.

## 3 RELATED WORK

**Few shot Image Classification:** Few-shot learning is a challenging problem in computer vision where the goal is rapid generalization to unseen tasks. Metric learning approaches such as (Snell et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2016; Sung et al., 2018) were some of the earliest approaches towards tacking this problem. (Snell et al., 2017) learn a metric space where prototypical representation of each category is utilized for classifying novel data. The prototypes are calculated to be the euclidean average over the support examples. (Ravichandran et al., 2019) showed that rather than taking the average, learning the prototypes along with the model can lead to better performance. More recently, a family of algorithms based on learning to learn (Andrychowicz et al., 2016) or meta-learning have gained considerable attention. (Ravi & Larochelle, 2016) develop an LSTM based meta-learner which learns the optimization algorithm required to train another neural network. (Finn et al., 2017; Nichol et al., 2018) create a model agnostic algorithm which aims to learn a good initialization that can be finetuned easily within a few gradient steps to adapt to new tasks. Hallucination based methods such as (Hariharan & Girshick, 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Antoniou et al., 2017) also present promising directions towards improved generalization. (Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018; Qi et al., 2018) try to directly predict the weights of the classifier for novel categories. (Yang et al., 2021) calibrate the distributions of few shot examples using the statistics of categories with larger number of examples. . However a recent line of work have questioned the need for metalearning or metric learning approaches and showed that simple baselines which are non-episodic in nature can provide competitive performance for few-shot image classification task (Chen et al., 2019; Dhillon et al., 2019). Such line of work provide for non-sophisticated baselines and pose a question to the community to rethink the approach towards few shot learning.

**Adversarial examples:** Adversarial examples are carefully crafted perturbations designed to fool the model (Szegedy et al., 2013; Goodfellow et al., 2015; Kurakin et al., 2016). (Goodfellow et al., 2015) showed that adversarial examples can be created rather easily using the sign of single gradient step, which they called as Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM). The existence of such examples raises a

question regarding the generalization capabilities of Deep Neural networks while also posing a threat to practical deployment. Many defenses have been proposed to overcome this problem (Papernot et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2017; Feinman et al., 2017; Li & Li, 2016), but they have been bypassed with slight modifications to the adversary (Athalye et al., 2018; Carlini & Wagner; 2017). One of the most common approaches called adversarial training involves incorporating the adversarial examples into the training set. (Madry et al., 2018) showed that the first order adversary, based on the Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) algorithm can be used to train robust neural networks. Provable methods have developed which can provide certification on the susceptibility of an input towards adversaries (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Wong et al., 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018; Gowal et al., 2018). A class of algorithms based on randomized smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020) have shown promising results in training large scale neural networks. (Zhang et al., 2019a) provide a theoretical analysis on the robustness vs accuracy trade-off, which had been studied empirically (Tsipras et al., 2018), and show that their algorithm named TRADES improves robustness compared to previous approaches. (Shafahi et al., 2019) use the gradients from the backpropagation algorithm to improve robustness with minimal cost.

**Adversarial Robustness for Few-shot classifiers:** Recent works have tried to address the problem of adversarial examples in the context of few-shot learning. (Yin et al., 2018) was one of the first works to discuss this problem. They used the FGSM adversary to create adversarial examples and optimized a meta-learner to be robust to adversarial examples. (Goldblum et al., 2019) showed that meta-learning algorithms can be supplemented with adversarial examples in the query set to learn robustness. Their method called Adversarial Querying was shown to be robust to strong attacks such as PGD. Many meta-learning approaches were extended to their robust counterparts by including adversarial query examples. Recently, (Wang et al., 2021) also proposed a similar approach where MAML was used as the base meta learning algorithm. (Wang et al., 2021) also showed that including a contrastive learning objective similar to (Chen et al., 2020) can provide a way to use unlabelled data when learning the model and thus improve both standard and robust accuracy.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we describe our experiments and provide implementation details. We consider three benchmark datasets - **Mini-ImageNet** , **CIFAR-FS** and **CUB** for our experiments. **Mini-ImageNet** consists of 100 classes derived from ImageNet dataset (Russakovsky et al., 2015) where each category consists of 600 images. This was first proposed in (Vinyals et al., 2016) and recent works follow the split provided by (Ravi & Larochelle, 2016) consisting of 64 base, 16 validation and 20 novel classes. We use 84x84x3 images for all experiments with Mini-ImageNet. **CIFAR-FS** was proposed in (Bertinetto et al., 2016) as a benchmark for few-shot classification. It splits CIFAR-100 dataset similar to Mini-ImageNet and we use 32x32x3 images for all our experiments. **CUB** (Wah et al., 2011) is a fine-grained dataset which has been used as a benchmark for few-shot classification. It consists of 11788 images split across 200 categories. We use the split provided by (Hilliard et al., 2018) consisting of 100 base , 50 validation and 50 novel classes. We use 84x84x3 images for experiments with CUB and as per our knowledge are the first to show adversarial robustness for a fine-grained dataset under few-shot setting. We use Pytorch (Paszke et al., 2017) framework and all our experiments use NVIDIA 2080Ti and Titan RTX GPUs.

**Implementation details:** In the base training stage, as described in Section 2.1, we follow the attack parameters of (Goldblum et al., 2019) and use iterative PGD attack with 7 iterations during training with _ϵ_ = 8 _/_ 255 and _α_ = 2 _/_ 255 for all experiments unless otherwise mentioned. We mainly use the standard network architecture ResNet (He et al., 2016) and also provide results for other architectures. For weight averaging, we set the parameter _τ_ = 0 _._ 999. We use SGD optimizer with a learning rate of 0.1 and weight decay of 1 _e −_ 5 for the feature extractor parameters _θb_ and 1 _e −_ 4 for the classifier parameters _ωb_ . We train the model for 250 epochs with a batch size of 64. For novel training and learning the Linear Classifier, we follow the setting described in (Chen et al., 2019) and learn the parameters using SGD with momentum 0 _._ 9 and learning rate _η_ = 0 _._ 01. We set the dampening as 0 _._ 9 and weight decay of 1 _e −_ 3. For our Mean Calibration, we use _m_ = 2 number of base categories. For calculating Robust Accuracy, we use 20 iterations of PGD to create an adversarial example and measure accuracy w.r.t ground truth. We report both Standard Accuracy and Robust Accuracy for 5-way, 1-shot and 5-way, 5-shot settings averaged over 1000 different trials on the test set. We report the mean of the different trials as well as the 95% confidence intervals for all our experiments. Since our goal is to build robust models, we mainly focus on improving Robust Accuracy.

We compare our results with (Goldblum et al., 2019) ,which we refer to as Adversarial Querying (AQ) where adversarial examples are created for query data and (Wang et al., 2021) which we refer as **OFA** where MAML (Finn et al., 2017) is used to adversarially train the model. We refer to training a linear classifier during the novel training stage as **Linear** and the Nearest Centroid classifier as **NC** .

## 4.1 MINI-IMAGENET

We present our results on Mini-ImageNet in Table 1. We observe that **NC** method outperforms other approaches in robust accuracy under most settings. We also show results on large-scale architectures such as WideResNets (Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2016) and DenseNets (Huang et al., 2017). We see that our NC method outperforms on Robust Accuracy in most settings and boosts standard accuracy as well. The difference becomes clear as we move to larger architectures. Our Linear classifier also serves as a strong baseline for robust few-shot settings.

Our method is straightforward to scale for large architectures since they are equivalent to training standard architectures. However, we observed that scaling meta-learning algorithms combined with PGD adversarial training is a difficult task. To provide a comparison, we trained both AQ and our model on 4 NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPUs using WideResNet-28-10 backbones. AQ method took 1.7 hour/epoch and required 60 epochs while our method took 0.36 hour/epoch for 250 epochs. The total training time for AQ was around 100 hours whereas our method took around 90 hours. This shows that our approach is more easily scalable compared to previous methods.

Table 1: **Results on Mini-ImageNet dataset** . Our NC method outperforms other approaches which becomes clear as we move to larger architectures. We can also see that our linear classifier serves as a strong baseline and can be used to learn robust few-shot classifier.

**Comparison with OFA:** We compare with another recent work OFA (Wang et al., 2021) where MAML was combined with adversarial training to improve robustness. We present them separately compared to previous results as the attack parameters and testing configuration followed are different. For a fair comparison, we use the same setting and we refer the readers to (Wang et al., 2021). **Base training** column indicates the type of adversary used during base dataset training where AT indicates PGD adversarial training, TRADES is the algorithm presented in (Zhang et al., 2019a) and CL corresponds to using the Contrastive Learning objective (Chen et al., 2020). Both TRADES and CL use additional unlabelled data in a semi-supervised manner. We observe from Table 2 that our method has clear gains in terms of robust accuracy and surpasses standard accuracy in some cases as well.

Since CUB is a fine-grained classification dataset, the base and novel categories share greater similarity compared to previous datasets. Hence it serves as an opportunity to understand how the robustness transfers from base to novel dataset, i.e whether the similarity in classes acts as a boon or bane under fine-grained dataset settings. As can be seen from our experiments, the linear classifier baseline performs reasonably well compared to previous approach indicating that a robust base classifier transfers to a robust novel classifier. The NC method also benefits under such settings and outperforms all other methods on both Standard and Robust Accuracy.

## 5 DISCUSSION

In this section, we provide additional intuition into our method and also showcase some benefits of our simple framework. We also refer the readers to appendix for more experiments and analysis.

**Understanding the effect of each component :** Our proposed approach consists of multiple components and we would like to study the effect of each of those. We perform an experiment on Mini-ImageNet dataset on ResNet18 backbone similar to Table 1 and the results are presented in Table 6. We observe that the simple baseline of robust base training and training a linear classifier during novel training works reasonably well which can be a considered a strong baseline (Exp Id 1,4). We also observe that DC algorithm proposed in (Yang et al., 2021) improves standard accuracy but introduces a drop in robustness (Exp Id 2,5). In most configurations we observe that our method matches or even outperforms the DC method and more importantly improves robustness (Exp Id 5,6). This shows that the robustness of the base dataset is transferred to the novel setting and the mean calibration along with Nearest Centroid classifier can improve robustness in few-shot setting. The impact of weight averaging (WA) method can be observed when considering Exp Ids 3 and 6. The temporal ensembling nature of the method helps in finding flatter minima, thereby boosting performance. Previous works (Gowal et al., 2020) have shown that this can be used for standard robust classifiers. Here, we observe this holds true under a transfer-learning type setting. We find that the combination of these methods can lead to improved performance.

Table 6: Illustration of different configurations of Base and Novel training. Here we show results on ResNet18 backbone on Mini-ImageNet dataset. WA represents Weight Averaging, DC represents Distribution Calibration and NC corresponds to to Nearest Centroid classifier.
**Extension to verifiably robust models:** An advantage of our simple framework is that we can incorporate methods from the adversarial examples literature for few-shot learning. Specifically,

we consider verifiably robust procedures where the goal is to provide a guarantee on adversarial robustness of the model. Standard adversarial training methods do not lead to provably robust models leading to low verified accuracy, we observe a similar trend in our experiments as well. Many methods have been proposed in this area (Mirman et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019b; Cohen et al., 2019) and we consider one of them - Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) (Gowal et al., 2018). Here we show that replacing PGD-training with IBP during the robust base training stage described in section 2.1 can lead to verifiable robustness for few-shot classifiers. We show results for 1-shot setting in Table 7 where we use a ResNet18 backbone on CIFAR-FS dataset. We use the training procedure as described in (Xu et al., 2020) with _ϵ_ = 8 _/_ 255 for 1000 epochs. Here Robust Accuracy refers to 20-iteration PGD testing and Verified Accuracy is calculated similar to (Gowal et al., 2018). We believe this experiment can encourage researchers to incorporate more advanced verification methods in the future and also develop algorithms specific to few-shot settings.

Table 7: Extension to verifiably robust classifiers. We show that it is possible to train verifiably robust models for few-shot settings. This is an added advantage of our framework due to the similarity to standard classifier training. Results are shown using ResNet18 backbone on CIFAR-FS dataset.

**Using Adversarial examples from few-shot categories:** We additionally consider an experiment where adversarial examples of the few-shot data are used when learning the linear classifier. Although this makes the learning more complex due to the adversarial training procedure, we perform this baseline experiment and learn the linear layer. As seen in Table 8 we observe a drop in performance which suggests that the network might be biased towards adversary of the few-shot data. We use a 7- step PGD and 20-step PGD when learning the classifier, similar to adversarial training. This suggests that creating adversarial examples increases computation but does not lead to performance gains. Our NC method does not include adversarial examples and outperforms previous methods without increasing computational burden. This experiment uses ResNet18 backbone on Mini-ImageNet dataset.

Table 8: Experiment where adversarial examples from few-shot data are included to perform adversarial training on the linear classifier. **No Adv** refers to clean examples used for learning the linear layer. **7-PGD** refers to 7-step PGD and **20-PGD** refers to 20-step PGD used in training. Robust Accuracy is calculated using 20-step PGD. Our NC method outperforms on both metrics without increasing computation due to adversarial training.

## 6 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK

We present a simple and scalable approach for improving robustness in few-shot image classifiers. Our method outperforms previous approaches when compared with standard few-shot learning benchmarks on both standard and robust accuracy. Note that our method is similar to traditional adversarial machine learning approaches rather than meta-learning methods, hence it becomes easier to introduce concepts such as certified robustness which is unexplored for few-shot classifiers. We show preliminary results in this direction and plan to explore this direction in future works. We believe that the simplicity of our approach would be beneficial to the community and upon which researchers can develop robust few-shot classifiers.

## 7 ETHICS STATEMENT AND REPRODUCIBILITY

We believe most AI algorithms including ours can be exploited by adversaries for unethical applications. On the positive side, we are developing algorithms that make deep networks robust to adversarial attacks, eliminating some of such undesired applications. Moreover, while publishing such defense algorithms may enable a larger community to access and benefit from them, leading to democratized AI, it can also enable adversaries to develop better attacks.

To ensure that our results are reproducible, we include all implementation details of our work and also provide code. We believe that readers will have sufficient knowledge of the hyperparameters associated with our method to reproduce the results.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely investigation into the intersection of adversarial robustness and few-shot learning, challenging the prevailing trend toward complex meta-learning frameworks in favor of a simpler transfer-learning baseline. The manuscript is well-motivated and clearly identifies a significant bottleneck in current research: the high computational cost and optimization instability of combining adversarial training with episodic meta-learning. By proposing a two-stage approach—standard adversarial training on base classes followed by a centroid-based classifier for novel tasks—the authors offer a scalable alternative that appears to achieve competitive results on benchmark datasets like Mini-ImageNet and CUB. However, the paper’s primary weakness lies in its limited theoretical or intuitive explanation of why a linear/centroid adaptation on novel classes should inherit the robustness of the base features without task-specific adversarial fine-tuning. While the empirical results are promising, the "centroid calibration" method mentioned in the abstract is insufficiently detailed in the introductory sections, leaving a gap in the technical narrative. To reach the high bar of ICLR, the authors must ensure the experimental section rigorously examines the trade-offs between clean and robust accuracy across varying shot counts (K=1 vs. K=5) and provides an ablation study to isolate the gains from the base robust training versus the specific centroid calibration technique.

comments.title: Mathematical Misalignment in NC Formula
comments.quote: $\mu_j = \frac{1}{m+1} (z_j + \sum_{i \in S_j} \mu_i^{(b)})$
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the author describes the Nearest Centroid (NC) calibration. The prose states: 'We find the nearest base-category centers to each novel training sample and then average them along with the novel training sample to obtain the new mean.' However, equation 2 (as reflected in the summary and text fragments like '$\mu_j = \frac{1}{m+1} (z_j + \sum_{...})$') defines the centroid $\mu_j$ for class $j$ based on a single sample $z_j$. For the $K$-shot setting where $K > 1$, the text later says: 'we calculate a centroid for each sample and average them to get one centroid for each category.' This procedure is mathematically different from $z_j$ being the mean of the $K$ support samples. If you average $K$ individual calibrated centroids, you are effectively weighting the base-class priors $m$ times more heavily than if you calibrated the mean of the $K$ samples once. Specifically, averaging $K$ calibrated centroids gives $\frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^K [\frac{1}{m+1}(z_{jk} + \sum \mu_i^{(b)})]$. This implies the base-class means $\mu_i^{(b)}$ are included $K$ times, potentially drowning out the novel sample signal as $K$ increases. The authors likely intended to calibrate the mean of the support set, or there is an ambiguity in how the summation is indexed across $K$ shots.

comments.title: Dimensionality Complexity Error
comments.quote: DC method can be computationally expensive due to the calculation of covariance matrix which can be of $O ( N * D^2 )$ complexity where $D$ is the dimensionality of the feature space and $N$ is the number of data points in the base dataset.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the authors argue against Distribution Calibration (DC) by citing its computational cost. They state the complexity is $O(N * D^2)$. However, calculating a covariance matrix from $N$ samples of dimension $D$ is typically $O(N * D^2)$ only if $N > D$. In modern deep learning (and specifically the backbones they use like ResNet18 or WideResNet), $D$ is often 512, 640, or even 2048. More importantly, the 'Distribution Calibration' method (Yang et al. 2021) actually fetches the pre-calculated statistics of the base classes. The complexity of calculating the covariance for a single novel class using the top-$m$ base classes as in DC is actually $O(m * D^2)$ or $O(m * D)$ depending on the specific implementation, which is independent of the total base dataset size $N$. While their NC method is indeed simpler, they likely overstate the 'expense' of the baseline they are comparing against by conflating the one-time offline cost of the base dataset with the online cost of novel task adaptation.

comments.explanation: In Section 2.1, the paper states they perform Weight Averaging (WA) and use $\theta_b'$ 'during the evaluation.' It is standard in robustness literature (e.g., Gowal et al., 2020) that WA significantly helps robust accuracy but can sometimes slightly degrade or have a complex relationship with standard accuracy. Following the results in Table 1, for WRN-28-10, 'Ours (Linear)' achieves 72.11% standard accuracy while 'Ours (NC)' achieves 70.13%. comments.explanation: The authors claim that certified robustness is 'unexplored' for few-shot classifiers. However, the paper's own summary and earlier method sections (Section 2) mention using IBP (Interval Bound Propagation) and 'Bosch/IBP methods' to produce verifiably robust models. While the authors likely mean it is unexplored in *prior* literature, the phrasing in the conclusion—combined with the fact that they claim to show 'preliminary results' in the very next sentence—suggests a potential overstatement if they are not the first to do so, or a slight internal contradiction if they consider their own current work (which they are concluding) as the evidence that it is now explored. Furthermore, references like Gowal et al. (2018) and Cohen et al. (2019) are cited in the related work, though they focus on large-scale data. If there truly are no prior works applying certification to the N-way K-shot setting, this claim holds, but usually, 'unexplored' is a strong term for a conclusion when a paper has just presented those exact results.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Mean Calibration' vs 'NC' performance claim
comments.quote: We present a simple method where we rely only on the calibrated mean and classify query sample using a non-parametric Nearest Centroid based algorithm... We do not consider the covariance matrix in our method and we find this approximation works equally well in our experiments.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the authors state that omitting the covariance matrix (Mean Calibration/NC) works 'equally well' as the full Distribution Calibration (DC) method. However, the formal summary of the paper claims that 'Mean calibration with NC is more effective at preserving robustness than Distribution Calibration (DC).' There is a slight inconsistency between claiming the methods are 'equally' good versus one being 'more effective.' If the NC method actually yields higher robust accuracy (as suggested in the Mini-ImageNet results summary), the phrase 'works equally well' in the method description might downplay their own finding or mislead the reader into thinking there is no performance trade-off at all, when the earlier claim suggests NC is actually superior for robustness.

In Section 2.2, they state that for novel training, the 'weights of feature extractor $f_{\theta_b}$ are frozen and not optimized.' While logically sound (one averages during base training, then freezes the result for novel tasks), the paper summary mentions $\theta_b' \leftarrow \tau \theta_b' + (1 - \tau) * \theta_b$. If the novel training involves a *Linear* classifier (which they describe as a baseline), the reader needs to know if the 'frozen' weights are the standard weights $\theta_b$ or the averaged weights $\theta_b'$. The text says 'use $\theta_b[']$ during the evaluation,' but does not explicitly confirm that $\theta_b'$ is the version used as the frozen backbone for training the novel linear classifier $C_{\omega_n}$. progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a conceptually simple yet compelling approach to adversarial robustness in few-shot image classification, advocating for transfer learning from an adversarially trained base model followed by centroid-based adaptation on novel tasks. While the idea of leveraging robust representations learned on base classes is intuitive and aligns with recent trends in transfer learning, the paper would benefit from substantially stronger methodological clarification and tighter presentation to match the high expectations of ICLR. Several critical issues undermine its current impact: first, the technical description is incomplete—Equation (1) and the surrounding mathematical formalism are cut off mid-sentence, leaving key components of the min-max optimization undefined. Second, the method section lacks sufficient detail on how exactly the centroid calibration is performed, what form the classifier takes during novel task adaptation, and how adversarial robustness transfers without retraining on few-shot examples. Third, although the paper emphasizes simplicity and scalability as strengths, it does not rigorously compare computational costs or training complexity with the meta-learning-based methods it claims to outperform. Additionally, while referencing relevant prior work in both few-shot learning and adversarial robustness, the paper misses a clear positioning of its core contribution—specifically, how its transfer-learning pipeline advances or fundamentally differs from prior art such as Chen et al. (2019) or Dhillon et al. (2019), beyond just applying adversarial training. As written, the work appears promising but under-realized, requiring more thorough experimental validation and clearer exposition to establish its scientific contribution with confidence.

comments.title: Missing Min-Max Objective Formula in Section 2.1
comments.quote: Specifically, we solve the min-max objective
comments.explanation: The text states that the authors solve a min-max objective for adversarial training but does not provide the actual equation. While it is standard in the robustness literature to refer to the min-max formulation introduced by Madry et al. (2018), the sentence sets up an expectation that the formula will follow immediately. In a well-structured paper—especially one focused on methodology—this is a natural place to formally define the objective. The absence of Equation 1 is likely an OCR or formatting error, since later references (e.g., to weight averaging update rules) suggest equations were intended. However, even accounting for possible truncation, the omission prevents immediate verification of the adversarial training setup (e.g., loss function, constraint type). This becomes more concerning given that the paper compares with methods using similar objectives. That said, context elsewhere—such as the mention of PGD and cross-entropy loss—resolves much of the ambiguity for a domain-expert reader. Still, the lack of formal specification here constitutes a minor gap in methodological clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Feature Extractor Parameters in Weight Averaging

comments.explanation: The authors state they apply weight averaging only to the feature extractor parameters _θb_, which aligns with their intent to preserve robust features for downstream adaptation. However, the update rule uses _θb_ on the right-hand side—presumably the current training iterate—and assigns to _θb[′]_, a shadow variable. This notation is mostly clear, but the use of _θb_ in both roles (as the main parameter and in the EMA update) could mislead a careful reader: is the EMA being applied to the same _θb_ used during training? The phrasing suggests yes, but standard implementations (e.g., SWA, EMA) decouple these variables. Moreover, the prime notation (′) is not defined, and the lack of distinction between _θb_ and _θb[′]_ in text (e.g., 'use _θb[′]_ during evaluation') risks confusion. Context resolves the intent: they are implementing EMA on _θb_, storing it in a separate buffer, and using that for evaluation—consistent with Gowal et al. (2020). Thus, while the notation is informal, it does not amount to a real error, but rather a mild underspecification.

comments.quote: Since we consider the normalized version of the vectors, euclidean distance reduces to the form in Equation 3, similar to recent works (Grill et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The paper states that ℓ² normalization reduces Euclidean distance to a simpler form (implied to be cosine or dot-product based) 'in Equation 3', but Equation 3 is not visible in the provided text. comments.title: Incorrect asymptotic complexity for sampling multivariate Gaussian
comments.quote: Moreover, sampling from a multivariate Gaussian with non-diagonal covariance is also expensive and can be of the order of at least _O_ ( _D_[2] _[.]_[3] ) (Bishop, 2006).
comments.explanation: The complexity is stated as O(D².³), which appears to be a misinterpretation or OCR artifact. Sampling from a multivariate Gaussian typically requires a Cholesky decomposition of the covariance matrix, which is O(D³), followed by a matrix-vector multiplication, also O(D²). Thus, the dominant cost is O(D³). While O(D².³) might refer to an optimized matrix multiplication algorithm (e.g., Strassen-like methods), it is not standard to cite this for Cholesky or sampling procedures. Bishop (2006) does not assert such a bound — the usual Cholesky-based sampling is O(D³). The authors’ concern about computational cost is valid, especially for high-dimensional features (e.g., D=512 or 1024), but stating O(D².³) instead of O(D³) inaccurately downplays the complexity. This may mislead readers about the actual scalability challenges. Given that this is used to justify a key design choice (dropping covariance), the justification rests on a questionable complexity claim.

comments.title: Underspecified weight averaging procedure for feature extractor
comments.quote: Similar to (Gowal et al., 2020), we keep a separate copy of the weights and for every iteration perform exponential moving average method _θb[′] ← τθb[′]_ + (1 _− τ_ ) _∗ θb_ and use _θb[′]_ during the evaluation.
comments.explanation: The procedure describes exponential moving average (EMA) on θb, but it is unclear when the update occurs: before or after the optimizer update? In standard practice (e.g., Gowal et al., 2020), EMA is applied after each optimizer step. The notation _θb_ likely refers to the current model weights post-optimization, but without explicit clarification, a reader might misinterpret whether θb is the pre- or post-update parameter. This matters because EMA applied to stale or intermediate weights can degrade performance. While the authors correctly cite and align with established practice, the lack of algorithmic ordering (e.g., 'after updating θb via SGD, update θb′') leaves the method underspecified. Given that EMA can significantly influence robustness and generalization, precise ordering is critical for reproducibility. This is especially relevant in adversarial training, where optimization dynamics are sensitive.

comments.title: Misstatement of adversarial training process
comments.quote: Once we find the perturbation, the perturbed input is added to the training set and parameters are tuned.
comments.explanation: This description inaccurately characterizes adversarial training. In standard practice (e.g., Madry et al., 2018), adversarial examples are not 'added to the training set' in the sense of expanding the dataset. Instead, for each minibatch, clean inputs are perturbed on-the-fly via PGD and used to compute the loss for that iteration — the original examples are not replaced or augmented permanently. The phrase 'added to the training set' suggests a static augmentation, which is not typical in PGD-based adversarial training and would be computationally prohibitive at scale. The correct interpretation is that adversarial examples are generated dynamically during training. While this may be a simplification for exposition, it risks misleading readers about the memory and sampling implications. The rest of the section correctly describes the min-max objective and PGD, so the high-level method is sound, but this sentence introduces a conceptual inaccuracy that could affect understanding of training dynamics or comparisons to data augmentation methods.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology: 'Distribution Calibation' vs 'Calibration'
comments.quote: **Background on Distribution Calibation (DC) (Yang et al., 2021):**
comments.explanation: The term 'Calibation' is clearly a typographical error for 'Calibration'. While this is likely due to OCR (as noted in the review instructions), it persists in the defined acronym 'DC', which would incorrectly stand for 'Distribution Calibation'. However, the surrounding context, including references to 'calibrated mean' and links to Yang et al. (2021), confirms the intended meaning. Since the error is superficial and does not affect technical understanding or reproducibility, it does not rise to the level of a substantive flaw. By the instructions, OCR-related typos that do not impair meaning should not be flagged unless they cause real confusion. In this case, the intended term is unambiguous from context.

comments.title: Unclear handling of square root transformation in centroid calibration
comments.quote: Note that similar to (Yang et al., 2021), as a preprocessing step, we transform the embeddings by taking the square root of each dimension so that their distribution gets closer to Gaussian form. It can be seen as “Tukey’s Ladders of Power Transformation” (Tukey et al., 1977) with \lambda = 0 . 5.
comments.explanation: The passage describes applying a square root transformation (i.e., \lambda = 0.5 in Tukey’s ladder) to embeddings as a preprocessing step. However, the centroid calibration formula (\mu_j = \frac{1}{|S_j| + 1} ( z_j + \sum_{i \in S_j} \mu_i^{[b]} )) and inference rule (\hat{y} = \arg\min_j \| \tilde{f}_{\theta_b}(x) - \tilde{\mu}_j \|_2) do not specify whether the transformation is applied before or after computing centroids or during inference. Crucially, if the transformation is applied only to features and not to centroids — or vice versa — then distances between transformed query features and untransformed centroids would be invalid. The intended procedure likely transforms both novel support features and base class means \mu_i^{[b]} before averaging into \mu_j, but this is not explicitly stated. While the intent may be clear to an experienced reader, the method is underspecified: a reproducer might misapply the transformation (e.g., only to \tilde f_{\theta_b}(x) at inference), leading to degraded performance. This ambiguity persists even when cross-referencing the PAPER SUMMARY.

comments.title: Missing justification for using non-robust hallucinated features in DC critique
comments.quote: Moreover, sampling from a multivariate Gaussian with non-diagonal covariance is also expensive and can be of the order of at least O( D[2] [.][3] ) (Bishop, 2006).
comments.explanation: The authors cite computational cost of sampling from a multivariate Gaussian as a motivation to avoid Distribution Calibration (DC). However, the more central claim — that DC leads to a 'non-robust final classifier' due to clean-feature bias — lacks justification. The argument hinges on the idea that hallucinated features are generated in feature space and thus cannot be adversarially perturbed. But the model being trained on these features is only ever exposed to clean features regardless — even without hallucination. The actual issue is not that hallucinated features can’t be perturbed, but that the classifier may fail to learn a decision boundary robust to perturbations because no adversarial versions of the original support set are used. This subtle point needs clarification. The critique is reasonable in spirit, but the reasoning conflates causality: it's not the hallucination per se but the lack of adversarial training on the novel task that causes fragility. A careful reader might be misled into thinking feature-space hallucination is inherently incompatible with adversarial robustness, which is not necessarily true if adversarial examples were generated in the latent space. The claim overstates the issue without sufficient justification.

comments.title: Ambiguity in m and its impact on centroid calibration generality
comments.quote: For our Mean Calibration, we use m = 2 number of base categories.
comments.explanation: The paper defines S_j as the set of m nearest base category centers to novel sample z_j, but sets m = 2 consistently in experiments. While this is a valid choice, the paper does not discuss sensitivity to m or justify why m=2 is optimal. More importantly, in a k-shot setting with k>1, it’s unclear whether m=2 is applied per sample (leading to potentially different S_j per support example) or globally. The centroid formula averages per-sample centroids, but if each z_j uses a different set S_j (which depends on k), then the calibration becomes asymmetric: samples from the same class may pull the final \mu_j toward different base classes. This could introduce variance unless S_j is shared across shots. The method section implies m is fixed and per-sample, but without clarification, a reader might not realize this design choice could affect reproducibility or generalization. The ambiguity is minor because context suggests m is a small fixed hyperparameter, but since the approach relies on nearest-centroid statistics, the choice merits discussion.

comments.title: Inference rule uses \tilde{\mu}_j but definition does not specify normalization timing
comments.quote: At inference time, we simply find the nearest center to the query point and assign its label: where \tilde{a} = a / ||a||_2 is the \ell^2 normalized version of the vector a.
comments.explanation: The inference rule uses \tilde{\mu}_j, implying that centroids are normalized, but the centroid calibration equation defines \mu_j before normalization. The question is whether normalization is applied to \mu_j after averaging or to each component (z_j and \mu_i^{[b]}) before averaging. The results could differ: normalizing after averaging preserves the calibrated mean’s direction; normalizing inputs before averaging could distort it. The paper states that \ell^2 normalization reduces Euclidean distance to cosine similarity — which is only strictly true when both vectors are normalized. Therefore, normalization must occur post-calibration. However, the algorithmic description does not explicitly state this ordering. Given the emphasis on efficiency, it's likely normalization is applied once to the final \mu_j, but this should be clarified. The context resolves most of the concern, but the risk of misinterpretation justifies a minor flag.

comments.title: Inconsistent robust accuracy comparison in larger architectures on Mini-ImageNet
comments.quote: We observe that **NC** method outperforms other approaches in robust accuracy under most settings. ... Our method is straightforward to scale for large architectures since they are equivalent to training standard architectures. However, we observed that scaling meta-learning algorithms combined with PGD adversarial training is a difficult task. To provide a comparison, we trained both AQ and our model on 4 NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPUs using WideResNet-28-10 backbones. AQ method took 1.7 hour/epoch and required 60 epochs while our method took 0.36 hour/epoch for 250 epochs. The total training time for AQ was around 100 hours whereas our method took around 90 hours.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the claim that 'NC method outperforms other approaches in robust accuracy under most settings' on larger architectures seems well-supported by Table 1, particularly for WRN-28-10 and DenseNets. However, concern arises when comparing training costs: AQ took 60 epochs (100h) while our method took 250 epochs (90h), implying AQ used a much more expensive per-epoch computation (1.7h vs. 0.36h). The comparison in Table 1 is only for ResNet18, WRN-50-2, WRN-28-10, etc., but the AQ results in the table are not stated to be from the same 60-epoch run on WRN-28-10. If the AQ results in Table 1 were from a different (presumably earlier) run with a smaller architecture or fewer resources, the speed/robustness comparison becomes misleading. Crucially, the table shows a drop in AQ's robust accuracy on WRN-28-10 (23.81%) compared to WRN-50-2 (23.04%)—but our NC method also sees a drop from 23.04% to 22.91%. Given the much higher per-epoch cost of AQ, one would expect larger gains with larger models, but the trend is reversed. The text suggests NC is more scalable, yet the evidence is mixed in the tables. The total runtime is comparable (100h vs. 90h), but our method uses more epochs with much cheaper iterations. The concern is not about correctness per se, but whether the performance and scalability claims are fairly contextualized. The paper could clarify whether the Table 1 results for AQ on WideResNets/DenseNets are from the same runs as the timing experiment. Otherwise, a reader might conclude NC is both faster and more accurate, when the reality may involve trade-offs.

comments.title: Potential confusion in NC method’s formula absence and definition
comments.quote:
More formally:

where _µj_ is the center for the novel category _j_ and _Sj_ is the set of _m_ base category centers that are closest to _zj_ , _µ[b] i_[is the mean of base category] _[ i]_[ in the feature space.][In the case of] _[ k]_[-shot setting, we] calculate a centroid for each sample and average them to get one centroid for each category.
comments.explanation: The text says 'More formally:' and then provides only prose, not an equation. This is a clear omission. The core of the Mean Calibration method—averaging novel sample features with the _m_ nearest base class centers—lacks a mathematical formulation. While the description is understandable, the absence of an equation (e.g., $ \mu_j = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^K \frac{z_j^{(k)} + \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i \in S_j^{(k)}} \mu_i^b}{2} $ or similar) makes reproduction ambiguous. For instance, is the average over samples done before or after merging with base centers? The text says 'calculate a centroid for each sample and average them,' but it doesn’t specify whether each support sample gets its own set of _m_ nearest base centers, or if base centers are shared across samples of the same novel class. Given that $ m=2 $ is a key hyperparameter and the method builds on Yang et al. (2021), a formal expression is expected. The prose is plausible and matches the intent described, but for a method presented as a contribution, the lack of a formula is a reproducibility gap.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Nearest Centroid inference under ℓ² normalization
comments.quote:
At inference time, we simply find the nearest center to the query point and assign its label:

where ˜ _a_ = _a_[Note that] _||a||_ 2[is the] _[ ℓ]_[2][ normalized version of the vector] _[ a]_[ and][ ˆ] _[y]_[ is the predicted category.] _ℓ_ 2 normalization is performed for both query point and centroids. Since we consider the normalized version of the vectors, euclidean distance reduces to the form in Equation 3, similar to recent works (Grill et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The text states that ℓ² normalization reduces Euclidean distance to a form 'in Equation 3'—but Equation 3 is not visible in the provided passage. However, context suggests it refers to cosine similarity: for ℓ²-normalized vectors, minimizing Euclidean distance $ \|\tilde{x} - \mu_j\|_2^2 $ is equivalent to maximizing cosine similarity $ \tilde{x}^\top \mu_j $. This is standard and correct. The real ambiguity is whether the Nearest Centroid classifier uses Euclidean distance on normalized vectors or directly computes cosine similarity. The claim that 'euclidean distance reduces to the form in Equation 3' is technically correct but could mislead if Equation 3 is not explicitly defined. A careful reader might wonder if normalization is applied per comparison or globally. However, since the paper cites Grill et al. (2020), where this equivalence is standard, and since ℓ² normalization is stated for both, the interpretation is recoverable. Yet, given that this is a core inference mechanism, explicitly stating the equivalence (e.g., 'thus nearest centroid under ℓ² distance is equivalent to nearest neighbor under cosine similarity') would prevent any ambiguity. The issue is minor because the meaning is inferable from context and common practice.

comments.title: Missing specification of NC variant in Table 1: no TRA or CL variants shown
comments.explanation: Table 1 compares AQ with 'Ours (Linear)' and 'Ours (NC)', all under AT (PGD adversarial training) as implied by implementation details. However, in Section 4.1, the paper notes that 'Both TRADES and CL use additional unlabelled data'—but Table 1 contains no such variants for the proposed NC method. The comparison is thus incomplete: the proposed method is evaluated only under AT, while baselines like OFA in Table 2 are shown under TRADES and CL. This creates a potential imbalance. The authors later state that they 'conduct an experiment using TRADES during Base Training', but the results are only given in Table 4 (CIFAR-FS), not Mini-ImageNet. A reader may question whether NC truly improves generalization across robust training methods if results are not shown consistently. While the paper's main proposal is to use NC with standard AT, the claim that 'our method can generalize to other adversarial training methods' would be better supported by symmetric evaluation across datasets and training schemes. This is not a flaw per se, but an underspecification of experimental coverage for a key claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of DenseNet results in robust accuracy claims
comments.quote: We observe that **NC** method outperforms other approaches in robust accuracy under most settings. ... Our NC method outperforms on Robust Accuracy in most settings and boosts standard accuracy as well. The difference becomes clear as we move to larger architectures.
comments.explanation: The claim that NC 'outperforms in most settings' and 'the difference becomes clear as we move to larger architectures' is generally supported — NC achieves highest robust accuracy on WRN-50-2, WRN-28-10, and ResNet18. However, on DenseNet121 and DenseNet161, while NC still leads in robust accuracy (20.21% and 20.63% vs. AQ's 10.19% and 9.80%), the gap is primarily due to AQ's catastrophic drop in performance. In contrast, the linear baseline also surpasses AQ by a wide margin. This suggests that the issue may lie not with NC’s design, but with AQ’s incompatibility with DenseNet architectures under their training setup. The paper does not acknowledge this anomaly. Furthermore, NC does not clearly 'pull ahead' of the linear baseline on DenseNets — e.g., on DenseNet161 5-shot, NC achieves 33.87% robust accuracy vs. linear’s 27.11%, which is significant, but on 1-shot, the gain is only from 17.44% to 20.63%. The text implies that NC gains grow with model size, but on DenseNets, Linear already captures most of the gain over AQ. Thus, the narrative that larger architectures amplify NC’s advantage is not uniformly supported. This is not an error in data, but a questionable emphasis in interpretation.

comments.title: Missing square-root transformation in NC inference description
comments.quote: At inference time, we simply find the nearest center to the query point and assign its label: where ˜ a = a [Note that] ||a|| 2 [is the] ℓ 2 normalized version of the vector [ a] [ and][ ˆ] [y] [ is the predicted category.] ℓ 2 normalization is performed for both query point and centroids. ... Our inference can be considered similar to the Linear Classifier method except that our centres ( µj ) are estimated with simple averaging rather than learnt using SGD. Note that similar to (Yang et al., 2021), as a preprocessing step, we transform the embeddings by taking the square root of each dimension so that their distribution gets closer to Gaussian form.
comments.explanation: The passage states that a square-root transformation is applied to embeddings as a preprocessing step for Gaussianity, citing Yang et al., 2021. However, when describing the Nearest Centroid (NC) inference in Equation 3 (implied but not shown), there is no explicit mention that this transformation is applied to *both* the query point and the centroids *before* ℓ² normalization and comparison. This creates ambiguity: is the square root applied during training only, or also at inference? The method's reproducibility hinges on consistent preprocessing. While it's reasonable to assume the same transformation applies to both, the text does not affirm this. A careful reader might conclude the transformation is only for calibration (training), leaving inference undefined. The claim that NC 'works equally well' relies on consistent feature space assumptions. Without explicit confirmation, the method is underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of robustness in DC vs NC methods in discussion
comments.quote: We also observe that DC algorithm proposed in (Yang et al., 2021) improves standard accuracy but introduces a drop in robustness (Exp Id 2,5). In most configurations we observe that our method matches or even outperforms the DC method and more importantly improves robustness (Exp Id 5,6). This shows that ... the mean calibration along with Nearest Centroid classifier can improve robustness in few-shot setting.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify DC's robustness drop in Table 6 (Exp 2 and 5 vs others), and position NC as a robust alternative. However, the claim that NC 'improves robustness' relative to DC is misleading. NC improves over DC *not because it adds robustness*, but because it *avoids the fragility introduced by DC's hallucination process*. DC uses clean hallucinated features that cannot be adversarially trained, potentially biasing the classifier toward clean inputs. NC avoids this by design. The phrasing 'improves robustness' suggests NC actively enhances it, when it merely preserves base robustness. A more precise claim would be that NC maintains robustness better than DC while achieving high standard accuracy. This is a moderate overstatement, but context (the comparison in Table 6) resolves the core finding.

comments.title: Ambiguity in m in Mean Calibration definition
comments.quote: where µj is the center for the novel category j and Sj is the set of m base category centers that are closest to zj , µ[b] i [is the mean of base category] [ i] [ in the feature space.][In the case of] [ k]-shot setting, we calculate a centroid for each sample and average them to get one centroid for each category.
comments.explanation: The text defines m as the number of nearest base category centers used in Mean Calibration (m = 2 per PAPER SUMMARY), but the notation in the equation snippet is corrupted: 'µ[b] i' likely should be 'µ_b^i' (base class i's mean). The variable 'zj' is used but not defined. Given context, it likely refers to a novel support sample's embedding. However, the phrase 'closest to zj' implies m centers are selected per support sample. Then, 'in the case of k-shot setting, we calculate a centroid for each sample' suggests each support example has its own calibrated centroid using its m nearest base centers. But it's unclear whether (1) each support sample gets a separate calibrated mean, then averaged, or (2) the m nearest base centers are found once per novel class (e.g., using class centroid) and reused. The former is implied but computationally heavier and not clarified. The PAPER SUMMARY says 'm = 2 nearest base category centers used in mean calibration', suggesting a fixed m per class, not per sample. This ambiguity affects reproducibility: is m selected per sample or per class?

comments.title: Unjustified exclusion of adversarial training during novel adaptation
comments.quote: we use only clean examples and not adversarial examples, making the process straightforward. ... One challenge associated with using few-shot data is that the model can become biased towards the specific samples and may not capture the true class distribution.
comments.explanation: The authors argue against adversarial training during novel adaptation, citing risk of overfitting and 'forgetting' robustness. They later show in Table 8 that adversarial training on the linear layer hurts performance. However, this is only tested on Mini-ImageNet with ResNet18. For datasets like CUB or with larger models (WideResNet, DenseNet mentioned later), this may not hold. The argument assumes that base robustness generalizes perfectly to novel tasks, which may not be valid under distribution shift or fine-grained differences. While freezing the backbone is justified, completely excluding adversarial examples in novel adaptation is a significant design choice. The paper doesn't test whether *small-step* or *regularized* adversarial training on novel data could help without overfitting. This is not an error per se, but an underspecified justification — they show it doesn't work in one setting, but claim broad generality.

comments.title: Inconsistency in standard accuracy leadership on CUB 5-shot
comments.explanation: In Table 5, Ours(NC) shows the highest standard and robust accuracy across all settings. However, in the preceding text, the authors state: 'The NC method also benefits under such settings and outperforms all other methods on both Standard and Robust Accuracy.' This is correct for the data shown. But earlier in the text, they say: 'the linear classifier baseline performs reasonably well compared to previous approach' — this is ambiguous. 'Previous approach' likely refers to AQ, but AQ has lower standard accuracy in 1-shot (54.27 vs 51.93). So Linear is *not* better than AQ on standard accuracy, contrary to what 'reasonably well compared to' might imply. However, Table 5 shows Ours(NC) is best overall. The phrasing could mislead a reader to think Linear outperforms AQ, but numbers show otherwise. Given the table immediately follows, this is minor and resolved by data.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about certified robustness in conclusion
comments.quote: We show preliminary results in this direction and plan to explore this direction in future works.
comments.explanation: The passage claims that the authors 'show preliminary results' toward certified robustness (e.g., via IBP or randomized smoothing), and position this as a benefit of their method’s simplicity. However, in the provided paper context—including claims, experiments, and methodology—no actual results on verifiable or certified robustness have been presented. While the abstract and paper summary do state that 'Replacing PGD with IBP in base training enables verifiable robustness' and that the method supports IBP integration, these claims are not backed by experimental results in the provided text (e.g., no verified accuracy numbers are included in the discussion or tables). The mention in the conclusion gives the impression that such results have already been demonstrated, which overstates what has been shown. Context does confirm that IBP is discussed as a feasible extension, but the phrase 'we show preliminary results' suggests empirical demonstration rather than mere feasibility, which is not supported by available information.

comments.title: Ambiguity in reproducibility due to missing implementation details for Weight Averaging scheduling
comments.quote: We perform exponential moving average method _θb[′] ← τθb[′]_ + (1 _− τ_ ) _∗ θb_ and use _θb[′]_ during the evaluation. We set _τ_ = 0 _._ 999 in all our experiments.
comments.explanation: The method section describes Weight Averaging (WA) using an exponential moving average of feature extractor weights with τ = 0.999. However, it does not specify when the moving average is initialized (e.g., from the start or after a certain epoch), whether it is updated every iteration or only after each epoch, or how _θb[′]_ is initialized. While similar practices exist in prior work (e.g., Gowal et al., 2020), omitting these details makes reproduction ambiguous. The paper summary confirms that WA improves robustness, and the experiments claim both standard and robust accuracy gains, so the correct application of WA is critical. Although the momentum value is given, the exact update schedule and synchronization with training steps are underspecified, risking performance discrepancies upon reproduction.

comments.title: Potential conflation of standard and robust few-shot evaluation protocols in related work
comments.quote: (Goldblum et al., 2019) showed that meta-learning algorithms can be supplemented with adversarial examples in the query set to learn robustness. Their method called Adversarial Querying was shown to be robust to strong attacks such as PGD.
comments.explanation: The related work section attributes strong PGD robustness to Adversarial Querying (AQ) without clarifying the evaluation setting. In the context of few-shot learning, robustness must be evaluated not only on attacks during inference but also with respect to the adaptation procedure. The paper summary notes that 'using adversarial examples during linear classifier training does not improve performance and may degrade results,' which suggests that injecting adversarial examples in the query set (as AQ does) may not be as effective as claimed. While Goldblum et al. (2019) may indeed report such results, the present paper's own evidence challenges the effectiveness of AQ. This creates tension: the text praises AQ for robustness, but their experiments show NC outperforming AQ. Without a qualifier (e.g., 'as reported in AQ'), this risks appearing as an unqualified endorsement rather than a neutral citation.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper presents a conceptually simple yet compelling approach to adversarial robustness in few-shot image classification, advocating for transfer learning from an adversarially trained base model followed by centroid-based adaptation on novel tasks. While the idea of leveraging robust representations learned on base classes is intuitive and aligns with recent trends in transfer learning, the paper would benefit from substantially stronger methodological clarification and tighter presentation to match the high expectations of ICLR. Several critical issues undermine its current impact: first, the technical description is incomplete—Equation (1) and the surrounding mathematical formalism are cut off mid-sentence, leaving key components of the min-max optimization undefined. Second, the method section lacks sufficient detail on how exactly the centroid calibration is performed, what form the classifier takes during novel task adaptation, and how adversarial robustness transfers without retraining on few-shot examples. Third, although the paper emphasizes simplicity and scalability as strengths, it does not rigorously compare computational costs or training complexity with the meta-learning-based methods it claims to outperform. Additionally, while referencing relevant prior work in both few-shot learning and adversarial robustness, the paper misses a clear positioning of its core contribution—specifically, how its transfer-learning pipeline advances or fundamentally differs from prior art such as Chen et al. (2019) or Dhillon et al. (2019), beyond just applying adversarial training. As written, the work appears promising but under-realized, requiring more thorough experimental validation and clearer exposition to establish its scientific contribution with confidence.

comments.title: Missing Min-Max Objective Formula in Section 2.1
comments.quote: Specifically, we solve the min-max objective
comments.explanation: The text states that the authors solve a min-max objective for adversarial training but does not provide the actual equation. While it is standard in the robustness literature to refer to the min-max formulation introduced by Madry et al. (2018), the sentence sets up an expectation that the formula will follow immediately. In a well-structured paper—especially one focused on methodology—this is a natural place to formally define the objective. The absence of Equation 1 is likely an OCR or formatting error, since later references (e.g., to weight averaging update rules) suggest equations were intended. However, even accounting for possible truncation, the omission prevents immediate verification of the adversarial training setup (e.g., loss function, constraint type). This becomes more concerning given that the paper compares with methods using similar objectives. That said, context elsewhere—such as the mention of PGD and cross-entropy loss—resolves much of the ambiguity for a domain-expert reader. Still, the lack of formal specification here constitutes a minor gap in methodological clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Feature Extractor Parameters in Weight Averaging
comments.quote: we perform weight averaging for only the feature extractor parameters _θb_ ... keep a separate copy of the weights and for every iteration perform exponential moving average method _θb[′] ← τθb[′]_ + (1 _− τ_ ) _∗ θb_
comments.explanation: The authors state they apply weight averaging only to the feature extractor parameters _θb_, which aligns with their intent to preserve robust features for downstream adaptation. However, the update rule uses _θb_ on the right-hand side—presumably the current training iterate—and assigns to _θb[′]_, a shadow variable. This notation is mostly clear, but the use of _θb_ in both roles (as the main parameter and in the EMA update) could mislead a careful reader: is the EMA being applied to the same _θb_ used during training? The phrasing suggests yes, but standard implementations (e.g., SWA, EMA) decouple these variables. Moreover, the prime notation (′) is not defined, and the lack of distinction between _θb_ and _θb[′]_ in text (e.g., 'use _θb[′]_ during evaluation') risks confusion. Context resolves the intent: they are implementing EMA on _θb_, storing it in a separate buffer, and using that for evaluation—consistent with Gowal et al. (2020). Thus, while the notation is informal, it does not amount to a real error, but rather a mild underspecification.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Mean Calibration Equation Definition
comments.quote: where _µj_ is the center for the novel category _j_ and _Sj_ is the set of _m_ base category centers that are closest to _zj_ , _µ[b] i_[is the mean of base category] _[ i]_[ in the feature space.][In the case of] _[ k]_[-shot setting, we] calculate a centroid for each sample and average them to get one centroid for each category.
comments.explanation: The description of the Nearest Centroid (NC) method is conceptually sound and closely follows Yang et al. (2021), but the presentation suffers from OCR-induced fragmentation. Phrases like 'µ[b] i_[is the mean of base category]' suggest that a formula was garbled—likely intended to be something like '_µ_i^[b]_ is the mean of base category _i_'. Similarly, '[In the case of] _[ k]_[-shot setting, we]' appears to be fragmented prose that should state how per-sample centroids are averaged in the _k_-shot case. Despite this, the overall procedure is reconstructable: for each novel support sample _z_j_, find _m_ nearest base class means, combine them with _z_j_ via averaging to calibrate the novel prototype. The algorithm is further clarified in prose and inference description. However, due to the OCR corruption, a reader cannot precisely confirm the aggregation rule—e.g., is it a simple average of _z_j_ and the _m_ base centers? The omission of mathematical parentheses or summation notation adds ambiguity.
